item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like this one. I can see the comparisons to SCP-900 but I kinda prefer this. Unlike a lot of SCPs, this one isn't overtly malevolent or an artifact of impending doom but more of a double-edged sword in that it's quite possibly a shield of sorts from so many of the other dangers threatening to unravel the threads of reality. This comes at the expense of not knowing the full nature of life as one of the intangible residents and not knowing how much free will is retained or whether Dahut is lying.

Would be better with more detail of the city and structures but that can be addressed with a future edit/update.

item.title: No vote
item.content:encoded:
…So professional written, so much professional words.
Unfortunately but I won't vote this article, I dislike the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wondered if somebody would adapt that myth… I like that story and I like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's heartwarming and heartbreaking. So cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hrm.

Well easy critique first, the run-on sentence error I mentioned in chat happens a few more times, and there's some internal tense issues, which didn't endear me very much. I like the idea, though, which pulls at me until I get to the -3 interview.

I like the writing in the interview, but…I don't know, the conflict, the thing that's driving the story of this article, is made too explicit and almost too…trite? for me to upvote. Like, a sanctuary from war? Is that it? It's almost an anti-conflict-we're seeing the aftermath of the great stand against political power and arrogance, and all the conflict is just gone. But there totally could be conflict. Like, what are the implications for this place now that the SCP Foundation—the foremost symbol of immense power used to protect-is now knocking on the front door? Shouldn't that impact the story more?

neutral vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only reason I upvoted this was because I love the queen character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm curious why names that are █████████ in the text, are [REDACTED] when SCP-1069 it is speaking? Is it using a different word that must be redacted or simply a different format for the interview?

It's meant to show that -3 called the researcher by first name, though I guess that isn't clear at all.

*ponders*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm neutral on this one. It's very well written, but as you say, mostly an adaptation of existing mythology. I never read the original so I can't compare, but I don't really see that much of a heavy twist on the myth. Lies and vilification of pagan figures by people in authority is a common theme. But it's always nice to have a story where you don't know who to trust (reminds me of the movie Black Death that I saw recently).

I'm curious why names that are █████████ in the text, are [REDACTED] when SCP-1069 it is speaking? Is it using a different word that must be redacted or simply a different format for the interview?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rewritten.

In hindsight, this article was precisely what I tell newer members not to do with a myth or legend-based SCP; that is, a straight-up adaptation in SCP format. Now, it's a heavy twist on the myth of Ys and hopefully far more interesting.

Beware the web of lies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just…bland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty much what everyone else is saying (apart from SCP-900 comparisons) - this just feels incomplete. I want to like it, really I do, but…it doesn't have a real hook or anything that even made me raise my eyebrows (apart from hallucinations engaging in "hedonistic behavior" with no further description - was that thrown in just because this is SCP-ten-sixty-nine? Feels like it was…).

To be more helpful, all of the anomalous aspects of this SCP are okay-ish - nothing stellar, but nothing really horrible, either; and I also nod at the "real-world" part of the description where it talks about what non-anomalous history can be gathered from the ruins, that's really nice and helps ground everything, no pun intended. The thing is, the article needs grounding, because it just feels kind of disjointed, the anomalous qualities (which, again, on their own, aren't bad) almost barely related to each other…It feels like "mysterious ancient city with indications at super-advanced building technology" done relatively well considering the unremarkableness of that concept, with "images of people who aren't really there" tacked on and "demonic features" tacked onto that tacked on bit just for creepiness's sake, references to a succubus-type entity thrown in there to add more creepiness for creepiness's sake, oh, and by the way, those two doctors who went down there in the submersible (which a previous commenter says shouldn't have even been openable while it was underwater) and who vanished but left their clothes behind have been seen among the hallucinations, maybe the "regal female entity" is gathering souls, woooOOOOOOoooOOOOO!

I'm not good at speaking normal human. -_- What I'm trying to say is that the foundation (no pun intended) of this scip - anomalous underwater city - is weak, and the things that build on it feel completely unrelated to it and therefore aren't even supported by that, so they're just kind of floating…It feels messy and disjointed and non-sequitor, does that make sense?

…Okay, suggestion time, I really suck at explaining my thought processes. The article mentions that the city might have been destroyed by an invading force opening the flood gates and allowing a tsunami to hit the interior of the city. Do something with that. Give the hallucinations some connection to that particular part, so they make more sense being part of the scip. It might be cliché to change "female hedonistic hallucinations with 'demonic features' when viewed peripherally" to, say, "hallucinations of people wrapped in rags and seaweed who look battered and drowned with 'demonic features' when viewed peripherally", but go somewhere with the idea - otherwise, it's pointless, and actually drags the article down.

Actually, come to think of it, maybe just remove the bit about the city being destroyed by a tsunami, make the city's collapse unknown or even [REDACTED]. That may sound like a stupid idea (and I do like the real history bit), but if the foundation of the thing was "mysterious city with possible advanced technology" without the "catastrophic destruction by giant wave", the hedonistic hallucinations would feel less out-of-place. Explaining what sort of catastrophe wiped out a city sets up the expectation that the hallucinations that come into play later will in some way relate to that catastrophe, and result in them feeling completely non-sequitor when they don't.

…Gah. *pounds forehead on desk* Could someone please read through this comment and translate it into something that might make sense? I'm trying to be helpful, I really am, but I just don't know how to explain what I'm thinking. I don't speak human!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Downvote removed, but nothing really sticks out that makes me want to upvote. It's just kinda there.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is being placed up for review. If you can read it, that would be great, because we feel it could use more attention.

I think the base idea of this, which is why I initially upvoted this many moons ago, but the execution doesn't do much for me now. The description didn't really have enough detail to rouse my interest, although what's there is a very good start. The addenda kinda killed it for me though, the dialog wasn't very good and the addenda building on it were pretty cliche. If they could be cut in favor of more interesting flavor text about the city itself, that would kick ass. As it is, neutral voted. I eventually downvoted, the potential is there but this doesn't do enough to make it good for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm inclined to agree with those who think that this is too similar to SCP-900 to be enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found the transcript a little confusing- didn't at first realise there were two Dr (blanks) even though the blanks were different lengths. Perhaps an initial or something with the rest blanked out would be clearer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this just enough to upvote. Wish it had more interesting details, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"in the eastern district of SCP-1000. Personnel attempted to communicate with these entities, but were unable to elicit a response."

Missed a spot.

upvoted. :-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this one quite a bit more than 900, it just seems more…SCP-ish

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it better when it was a monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reasonably sure that submersible hatches all open outward, specifically so it's literally impossible to open them while under pressure. Airplane exits open inward for the same reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 900's better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
also one unknown language and one female figure who speaks to staff is enough

among other similarities

i'm pissed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is much too similar to SCP-900 for me. It's also not nearly as good.

One sunken city of the coast of wherever where you have auditory hallucinations of the city being inhabited is plenty, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that I've tried to upvote this multiple times says something about how well written this is.
Didn't realize there was another SCP so similar to this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Before anyone gets bent out of shape about the 1000 block not being open yet, Mackenzie had earned a slot in the contest, and this is an update of that same entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I promised I wouldn't make a 69 joke here, so I'll just say that I like the tone, and I'm upvoting. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belated, but a major rewrite of my (personally disliked) SCP-1000 Proposal.
